entry.title: [CPI] HTTP "Don't Throw Exception on Failure" doesn't work properly on XI Sender Synch
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I have a synch XI Sender scenario, where the ERP sends a message to the CI, which makes an HTTPS request (with the option "Don't Throw Exception on Failure" enabled), and receives an HTTP Code 400 with a payload informing the reason for the error. I need delivery this message to ERP.
Analyzing the flow in the CI monitors, I don't see any errors, everything occurs as it should. However, when delivering the return message in the XI channel, an exception is generated on ERP, on proxy call.
This happens for example for HTTP Codes 400 and 404, but works perfectly for HTTP Code 500.
The payload received on 400 and 404, is shown on SXI_MONITOR as HTMLError, and no as MainDocument, as expected.

I think this is not happening as expected.
Has anyone noticed this behavior? Any known solutions?

Thanks,
Miguel Motta

entry.title: SAP Asset Performance Management - Manage Embedded IoT Metadata Sync
entry.content:
In this blog, I would like to outline how to read the connectivity status for a set of technical objects in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) - Embedded IoT.

Post the successful integration of  S/4HANA system with SAP APM, all the technical objects in SAP Asset Performance Management, with one or more indicators, are automatically synchronized to Embedded IoT platform to enable time series data ingestion. If we need to know the synchronization status of one or more technical objects including indicators, we can use READ Public APIs. Also, we can use POST Public API to manually retrigger a sync to the underlying IoT platform for a specific technical object if needed before the automatic scheduler run.

If you want to gain deep understanding of certain key functionalities in SAP Asset Performance Management (APM), i.e. Failure Curve Analytics and Achieving Sustainability with SAP APM through Industrial Systems, check out my previous posts below.

  1. Configuring Failure Curve Analytics in SAP APM 
  2. Achieving Sustainability using SAP APM through Industrial Systems 
  3. What`s New in SAP APM release 2502 

My Business Use Case: My Calcination plant has an Air Compressor(NL HZ) equipped with six key Indicators. I have created the equipment master in S4HANA system and replicated it to SAP APM. Now, I will use READ Public API to know the status of synchronization to the underlying IoT platform. Also, I will use POST Public API to manually re-trigger a sync to the EIoT platform for a "Submersible Pump-03 1.5HP 12 Stages".

First things first, ensure that the data replication toggle bar is turned ON in SAP APM.

Now, let us take a look at the Air Compressor and its Indicator data in SAP APM.

Air Compressor Indicators

I have set up the environment to use READ and POST public APIs for Technical Object & Indicator. SAP Has provided "Try Out" option for certain APIs to retrieve data from our own SAP APM system.

let us add the basic information to have the environment, e.g., Client ID, Host URL, Domain & Sub-Domain, etc.

 

 

Following the successful environment set-up, let us check Equipment 10001729 status.

 

 

Now, let us look at the technical objects and their indicators status. The value of $expand is only supported for indicators.

 

 

 

 

Now, let us take a look at the status of the Indicators.

 

 

 

POST Public API:

Fact Sheet: The following situations demand the usage of POST public API to manually re-trigger the sync to the EIoT platform.

  • When the automatic synchronization fails.
  • When we want to immediately sync a technical object to the underlying EIoT platform and not wait for the automatic scheduler to run, which runs once every 30 minutes.

Now, let me walk you through the POST public API to manually re-trigger the equipment "Submersible Pump-03, 1.5HP, 12 Stage" sync to the EIoT platform.

First let us create an Equipment in S4HANA system and sync it manually to the SAP APM system using "integration monitor".

 

 

Fact Sheet:

The master data, i.e., Equipment, Functional Location, Class, Characteristics, etc., synchronization between SAP S/4HANA and SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) occurs once in 2 Hours.

For Instance, I have manually triggered the equipment data sync to the SAP APM using Integration Monitor App.

 

Following the successful synchronization, system displays the equipment 10001778 in SAP APM.

Although Equipment 10001778 has been synced to the SAP APM, the indicator has not yet been onboarded to the underlying EIoT platform. When I attempted to add a new measurement reading  in SAP APM manually to the Indicator, system displayed the following error message. System takes 30 minutes to onboard the indicator to the underlying EIoT platform.

Now, let us manually re-trigger the Indicator sync to the EIoT platform using POST Public  API.

 

Once we run the POST Public API, system initiates the synchronization to the EIoT platform and prompts us to use  the GET public API to know the connectivity status.

Now, let us use GET Public API to know the connectivity status. To get the Indicator details, we must use the value of "$expand" for indicators.

 

 

Now , the system has synchronized the indicator successfully to the underlying EIoT platform, and enables to add indicator value.

 

 

😊This is all about understanding the synchronization status of one or many technical objects and their indicators using READ, POST public APIs. I hope my blog proves useful to my beloved maintenance team.

 

--

Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy.C

entry.title: Re: BAPI Displays user name as "COGI"
entry.content:
Dear Aditya

 

To change the user name that appears in the confirmation log, you must ensure that the BAPI is called explicitly within a dialog user session or via an RFC connection that uses a specific technical user. If you're calling the BAPI from middleware such as PI/PO or a background job, verify which user the RFC destination is using. Change the configuration to use the desired user ID, and that name will then appear in the confirmation record.

If you're executing the BAPI from a custom ABAP program, the user executing the program should be a dialog user. In this case, the system will log the SY-UNAME of the session, and it will appear in the confirmation details. Make sure your program is not calling any background job or queue-managed reprocessing logic, which may again default to "COGI".

If you're dealing with mass data processing and still want each posting to reflect the identity of a specific user, consider logging the original user in a custom field or using a custom enhancement to pass the original user name into a user-exit or BADI during confirmation. However, standard BAPI structures do not allow you to override SY-UNAME directly.

Best regards

entry.title: Need the ability to place released sales order back on credit hold in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Our client requires the ability to place a sales order that has been released through Documented Credit Decisions back on credit hold in public cloud.  They were able to do this in ECC on prem using VKM3 and reassigning the order back to credit hold.  Upon investigation it appears this functionality is not available in Public Cloud.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Need the ability to place released sales order back o...
entry.content:
API Sales Document with Credit Block - Read, Check, Release, Reject (A2X) can be used to trigger a same day credit recheck on the released sales order. The steps mentioned below should be followed to execute the API successfully:

  • Get the Credentials.
  1. Go to app Communication Arrangements.
  2. Find the arrangement for the scenario SAP_COM_0325 (Sales Credit Management Integration).
  3. The Communication User (Basic Auth) and Password (provided when the arrangement was created) can be found inside the arrangement. If the password is no longer known, a new Communication User and Arrangement may need to be created.
  • Test the API in Postman.

  1. In Postman, issue a GET request to:

    https://<your-tenant>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SLS_DOC_WITH_CREDIT_BLOCK/ 

    Add header: X-CSRF-Token: Fetch
    Use Basic Auth with the communication user from Step 1
  2. Copy the returned X-CSRF-Token and session cookies from the response.

  3. Run a POST request to:

    https://<your-tenant>/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SLS_DOC_WITH_CREDIT_BLOCK/CheckCreditBlock?SDDocumentCategory='C'&SalesDocument=''

    Add header: X-CSRF-Token: <token>
    Add header: Cookie: <your-session-cookie>

entry.title: Re: Manage processing rules - mass creation of rules
entry.content:
Hello @Constant 

Thank you for your question about creating bank statement processing rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in bulk.

The supported method for bulk creation is via the “Manage Bank Statement Processing Rules” app, using the Excel export/import feature. This is SAP’s official and recommended approach for both setup and replication across systems.

Please note.

  • LSMW and scripting tools are not available in the Public Cloud.
  • No public API exists for mass creation.
  • The Excel template is the closest equivalent to legacy upload tools.

If your system includes scope item 4X8 – Advanced Bank Statement Automation (standard from release 2408), it supports rule suggestions and enhanced mass maintenance.

SAP References

SAP Help Manage Processing Rules - For Bank Statements

KBA  3374309 - Processing rules in bank statement posting - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best regards,
Chuma

entry.title: Re: Sales order with service order versus revenue bearing...
entry.content:
Dear Sharky

Revenue-bearing service orders are best used when the service is the actual product being sold, such as repair, consulting, maintenance, or installation. In this case, the service order acts as both the operational and commercial document. It contains cost and revenue tracking, and is integrated with billing via the dynamic item processor. This model is ideal for service-centric organizations and supports time and material or fixed price billing without the need for a preceding sales order.

Sales orders with associated service orders are preferable when your business involves selling products, services, or bundled solutions like “equipment plus installation.” The sales order is used to manage the commercial agreement with the customer, including pricing, delivery schedules, and invoicing. The linked service order handles the execution of the service component. This separation provides clarity for both commercial and operational tracking.

If your process involves field service or onsite activities where the customer is primarily paying for labor or service execution, revenue-bearing service orders offer a more direct model. They simplify the end-to-end flow by consolidating service planning, execution, cost capture, and billing into a single document.

On the other hand, if you sell hardware along with warranty, delivery, and setup services, the sales order gives you better control over the commercial terms, especially when pricing varies across components. The linked service order ensures services are fulfilled, but the financial control remains at the sales order level.

Revenue-bearing service orders give better cost transparency at the operational level because the cost and revenue are both booked on the same document. This simplifies profitability analysis for services.

Sales orders with service orders distribute cost and revenue across documents, requiring more effort to track profitability at a detailed level. However, they integrate better with SD functionalities like availability check, shipping, and ATP.

From a billing perspective, revenue-bearing service orders can be billed based on time confirmation, material usage, or fixed price entries directly, without needing an SD billing document. This can streamline the invoicing process for service-only models.

Using a sales order as the commercial anchor is recommended when you need to combine multiple service orders under one invoice or contract. It allows you to maintain a structured sales hierarchy, helpful in B2B or large-scale projects.

In complex scenarios, you may even combine both models. For example, use a sales order for the commercial package and trigger internal revenue-bearing service orders for each service task, posting costs and revenues centrally. This hybrid approach is used in advanced service scenarios.

Ultimately, your decision should reflect whether the business is driven by service delivery or by product sales. If the customer is buying a service with measurable execution (e.g., hours worked, parts consumed), revenue-bearing service orders are optimal. If the customer is buying a product with attached service commitments, sales orders with service orders provide better structure and clarity.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: Make payment for co code ABC Supplier Invoices for sp...
entry.content:
Dear Gadkari

In your scenario, both company codes ABC and XYZ are defined as Paying Company Codes, and you're now facing a situation where you need company code XYZ to process payments on behalf of ABC — but only for specific Payment Methods (PMs). Since ABC is already marked as a Paying Company Code in FBZP, the system is not allowing you to define it as a Sending Company Code as well.

Here's how you can approach this requirement using standard SAP Payment Engine / FI-AP functionality:

Option 1: Use Centralized Payment with Cross-Company Payment Processing

SAP supports cross-company payments where one company code pays on behalf of another, and this is managed through intercompany clearing and FBZP configuration. However, it requires ABC to be set as a Sending Company Code, which is not possible if it's already set as a Paying Company Code. Since this dual role is restricted, here's a workaround:

Option 2: Use Alternative Payment Method + Payment Run Variant Logic

  1. Create a dedicated Payment Method (e.g., “Z”) in company code ABC.
  2. In the payment method configuration (FBZP) for method “Z”, do not assign a house bank in ABC.
  3. In payment run parameters, define a payment variant that:
    • Selects only invoices with method “Z”.
    • Is executed from company code XYZ.
    • Includes company code ABC invoices (multi-company payment run).
  4. In FBZP, allow cross-company code payment under payment method “Z”, linking ABC as a non-paying participant in this method.

Now, when you execute F110 from XYZ with this setup, it will:

  • Pick ABC’s open items with payment method Z.
  • Use XYZ’s bank account.
  • Book an intercompany payable/receivable between ABC and XYZ automatically.

Option 3: Use Payment Requests via FSCM or Payment Engine

If you're using SAP Payment Engine or FSCM-In House Cash, you can:

  • Post the original invoice in company code ABC.
  • Generate a Payment Request from ABC using a specific payment method or indicator.
  • Route the payment to be executed from XYZ’s Payment Engine account / house bank.
  • Handle the intercompany clearing either manually or through IHC auto-clearing.

Option 4: Post a Manual Journal Entry (not recommended for automation)

As a last resort, and only for exceptions:

  • ABC posts the supplier invoice.
  • XYZ posts a manual payment to the supplier, debiting ABC via a G/L clearing account.
  • ABC posts an intercompany clearing document to close the supplier liability and record the intercompany payable to XYZ.

This is manual and audit-sensitive, so it should be avoided in high volume cases

Best regards

entry.title: Re: Recovering from a large number of failed transport.
entry.content:
In SAP, when a large-scale transport causes failure and introduces instability, it's natural to think of a full system restore as the fallback. However, a full backup restore should always be the last resort because it impacts the entire system, not just the transport in question. Fortunately, there are other strategies available, though they depend on what kind of objects were transported and how your system is configured.

Here’s how to approach recovery without restoring the full backup:

1. Use SAP Version Management (SE39, SE80, SE03)

If your transport involved workbench objects (like ABAP programs, classes, dictionary objects):

  • SAP keeps versions of most development objects.
  • You can go into the object (e.g., ABAP program in SE38 or SE80), and use the Utilities > Versions > Version Management function.
  • Select the version prior to the transport and restore it.
  • Then re-transport that version to overwrite the faulty one.

This doesn’t apply to all object types, but it covers most ABAP artifacts.

2. Use Software Component Backup (CTS+) or gCTS (for ABAP Git)

If you’re using Change and Transport System Plus (CTS+) or gCTS, and working in a more modern landscape (e.g., S/4HANA + Git), you can:

  • Revert the Git repository (if using gCTS) to a previous commit.
  • Re-deploy only the stable version.
  • This gives you granular rollback, especially for ABAP development and Fiori/UI5 content.

3. Manual Rollback Based on Transport Contents

If versioning is not available or not sufficient:

  • Use SE10 / STMS to find the exact transport request.
  • Review its contents (objects, tables, etc.).
  • Manually revert configuration or master data changes using either:
    • Reference clients or templates (if configured),
    • Redoing the settings manually in SPRO (if Customizing objects),
    • Importing older transport requests that reflect the last stable state.

4. Use Customizing Transport Layers (Client-Specific Rollback)

If the transport was of Customizing (client-dependent) objects:

  • Identify the previous transport that had the correct configuration.
  • Re-import that request.
  • Optionally, use the "Import Again" function in STMS, and choose to reimport an older version into the affected client.

5. Selective Table Recovery (Advanced)

For advanced BASIS teams or with DB access and table logging enabled:

  • Some table data can be rolled back manually if you know the tables and changes involved.
  • This may involve writing custom scripts or using backup logs from DB level or change documents, table logging, or archived transports.

Note: This requires strong caution and typically not done in productive systems without extensive validation.

6. Shadow Systems or Retrofit (Dual Landscape)

If you're using dual-track systems (e.g., DEV/QA/Retrofit landscapes) like in ChaRM:

  • You can compare the stable system’s transport buffer or baseline to the current one.
  • Re-import clean versions selectively from the retrofit system.

7. Transport of Copies (ToC) Reimport

If a Transport of Copies was created for the original transport:

  • You can re-import the stable version using the ToC, provided it contains the uncorrupted state.

Final Thoughts

There is no universal undo button for SAP transports, but version management, previous transport re-imports, and manual correction provide ways to roll back changes without needing a full system restore.

entry.title: Re: CDS View for Project Budet,Actual And Commitment in S...
entry.content:
Dear Jagdish

You're aiming to perform budget validation during PR/PO creation with account assignment category 'Q' (Project), and you're looking for CDS views that expose budget, commitment, and actual values tied to WBS elements (used in 'Q' assignments). In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management (EPPM), this is a known challenge, as no single CDS view fully aggregates all three values.

Here’s a proven approach that combines multiple CDS views to achieve your requirement:

1. Budget Data

Use the CDS view:

  • I_ProjBudgetLineItemCube
    This cube provides approved budget amounts at the WBS element level. Fields include:
    • WBSElement
    • Project
    • ControllingArea
    • ApprovedAmountInObjectCurrency

For budget by fiscal year and version, consider also:

  • I_ProjBudgetLineItem

2. Commitment Data

Use:

  • I_PurchasingDocumentItemCommitmentCube
    This view gives commitment values from PRs and POs. Key fields:
    • WBSElementInternalID
    • CommitmentAmount
    • AccountingObjectType (should reflect account assignment Q)

If this does not include all types of commitments (e.g., from service entry sheets or scheduling agreements), you may also consider:

  • I_CommitmentLineItemCube (cross-functional, includes internal orders and WBS)

3. Actuals Data

Use:

  • I_ActualCostLineItemCube
    This cube provides actual postings (goods receipts, service entries, vendor invoices). Fields include:
    • WBSElement
    • ActualAmountInObjectCurrency
    • CostElement
    • PostingDate

4. Project Structure Reference

Use:

  • I_WBSElement or I_ProjectElement
    These views help resolve project hierarchy, WBS levels, and related organizational data. They allow joins with cost and commitment cubes via WBSElement.

5. How to Combine

You’ll likely need to build a custom CDS view or use SAP Analytics Cloud or Embedded Analytics to:

  • Join I_ProjBudgetLineItemCube, I_PurchasingDocumentItemCommitmentCube, and I_ActualCostLineItemCube on WBSElement.
  • Apply aggregation and group by fiscal year, WBS, and currency.
  • Filter for projects using account assignment category Q.

6. Alternative: BAdI-Based Validation

For enforcing validation logic at PR/PO creation:

  • Implement BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PR_PROCESS_ENHANCE or MM_PUR_S4_PO_PROCESS_ENHANCE
  • In the logic, call the CDS views (via an ABAP class or service layer) to read budget vs actual vs commitment.
  • Block or warn users if budget thresholds are exceeded.

Conclusion

There is no single CDS view today that provides budget, commitment, and actuals together for WBS/project usage. The best approach is a composite view or runtime join of the CDS cubes listed above. For validation logic, pair this data with BAdI implementation during document processing.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: 2lis_03_BF Setup table filling
entry.content:
Dear gvr 

 

The error message "Could not open file" during the setup table filling for extractor 2LIS_03_BF usually indicates a problem with file creation or access in the application server. When the setup table is filled using transaction OLI1BW, the system generates a sequential file to store intermediate data. If this file cannot be created, accessed, or written to, the process fails with this message.

The most common reasons include missing authorizations for file handling, incorrect logical file path configurations, locked or already open files, or file system access restrictions. Begin by reviewing the job log in transaction SM37 to find any further technical details. Then, check transaction AL11 to ensure the required directories on the application server exist and are writable. You should also validate logical file name settings in transaction FILE, especially those related to data archiving or extraction. Make sure that the user running the job has the S_DATASET authorization object with the correct activity types and logical file name values.

If you are running a recent SAP kernel or Support Package Stack, ensure that all required patches listed in SAP Note 2425796 are applied. In some cases, a missing or outdated patch may result in the file creation routines failing silently. If the issue persists, try to replicate the problem with a user that has SAP_ALL to eliminate authorization issues. If that works, then it's definitely a missing authorization. You can also try running the process in foreground to capture a more detailed error trace.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: Can we do value mapping through MDG Consolidation ?
entry.content:
Hello T,

1. Yes, this is SAP delivered standard adapter for value mapping which reads already maintained mapping data in MDG system. If no value is maintained, the value uploaded for consolidation process will be considered as is. i.e., if account group is ACCGRP1 and if no mapping is maintained, it will consider the value ACCGRP1. 

2. Once the value mapping is maintained in MDG, it will be considered for both inbound (when using the correct adapters) and replication from MDG to other systems. i.e., if value mapping is not in MDG for account group ACCGRP1, then during replication, same value will be considered.  

Including value mapping steps is one of the criteria to be considered for performance related consideration in CMP process. Exact system performance can be ascertained on individual system based on your load testing. 

Regards,
Saravanan

entry.title: Re: Auto printing issue while adding marketting documents
entry.content:
Hi

The issue you're experiencing where the AR Credit Memo automatically prints upon adding, despite the "Print When Adding" checkbox being unchecked, is likely due to a user-specific or layout-specific configuration rather than a global document setting. First, go to Administration > Setup > General > Users, select your user, and check the Print Preferences tab to see if there is a specific rule set to auto print for AR Credit Memos under the “When Adding” column. If that is checked, uncheck it and save.

Next, check if there is any active add-on, such as B1 Usability Package (B1UP) or any custom script, that could be triggering an automatic print event upon the addition of AR Credit Memos. Temporarily disable those add-ons and test again to isolate the behavior. Also, navigate to Administration > Setup > General > Report and Layout Manager and locate the AR Credit Memo layout. Verify if there’s any layout assigned as a default that includes embedded auto-print logic or associated actions that might bypass the system dialog.

Additionally, go to Administration > System Initialization > Print Preferences and review both the General and Per Document tabs. Sometimes, document-specific print behaviors are controlled at this level and may override global settings. If you are using SAP Business One Web Client, also check the Form Settings under that interface since the behavior can differ from the desktop client.

If the issue persists only for your user, create a new user without any specific print preferences and test the AR Credit Memo process again. If the problem does not occur with the new user, the issue is user-specific. If the problem is system-wide, then it may be related to the layout or background process triggered during document creation.

Lastly, if none of the settings seem to cause this, as a temporary workaround you can redirect the print to a virtual or dummy printer (e.g., Microsoft Print to PDF) until the root cause is identified. If you are using a layout tool like Crystal Reports, ensure there’s no embedded print function or script within the report that triggers upon execution.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: HU from EWM to ERP HU managed Sloc creates multiple r...
entry.content:
Hi

This behavior could be because when an HU is transferred from an EWM-managed storage location to an ERP storage location that is also HU and WM managed, the system treats it as two distinct logistic areas, even though the EXIDV (external HU ID) remains the same. Upon posting the Goods Issue (GI), the HU in the source location remains in status 0050 (In Transit) instead of being closed, and when the same HU is received in the destination storage location, a new record with the same EXIDV is created in VEKP, leading to multiple entries for the same HU number.

This duplication in VEKP confuses transactions like LM18, which are designed to read one active HU record per EXIDV. Since the HU now has multiple records with different statuses or locations, the standard function module behind LM18 might pick the first matching record, which may not be the one actually relevant for the transaction, especially if the older HU record is still in status 0050.

To resolve this, you must ensure proper HU closure after GI in EWM or manual cleanup of HU statuses in ERP. After GI posting, if the HU is not physically returning or reused, it should be set to status 0060 (Closed) to prevent system reuse or confusion. Alternatively, if the HU is still active and being reused, you need to update the original HU record instead of letting the system create a duplicate.

A recommended approach is to avoid creating multiple VEKP entries for the same EXIDV. This can be enforced by controlling the process through BAdIs or enhancement points at the interface between EWM and ERP, specifically at the point where the inbound delivery or putaway is processed. Also check if the system is configured to allow HU reuse or new HU number assignment upon receipt. Review the HUMO transaction to analyze HU lifecycle and use the report HUMO_DELETE_SINGLE_HU if needed to clean obsolete HU records.

Br

entry.title: Re: S4hana RISE on Private Cloud Vistex component
entry.content:
Dear Saurav

Once the Vistex component is removed during your S/4HANA 1909 to RISE Private Cloud conversion, you will not be able to access Vistex-specific tables (/IRM/*) or functionalities in the standard SAP environment. These tables and logic are part of the Vistex add-on, and without the component being installed, runtime references to /IRM/ tables will fail, and any dependency (e.g., in pricing conditions, agreements, historical orders) will be broken or inaccessible.

Standard SAP cannot interpret or render documents that rely on Vistex-specific structures if the corresponding add-on is not present. Therefore, orders that relied on Vistex logic for price determination or agreement reference might appear incomplete or not interpretable post-conversion unless precautions are taken.

To address this, here are your options:

  1. Archive the Vistex orders in 1909 before conversion: Use SAP ILM or ArchiveLink to archive documents with full Vistex metadata and pricing context. You can then access them in a read-only mode post-conversion via archive viewers. This avoids the need for the Vistex engine in the new system.

  2. Export Vistex-related order data: Before decommissioning Vistex, export all relevant order data (including pricing, agreements, and references from /IRM/* tables) into custom Z-tables or a sidecar HANA DB schema. Then create a custom Fiori or ABAP report in RISE to query this legacy data for historical analysis.

  3. Retain a legacy read-only system: Keep a copy of your S/4HANA 1909 system with Vistex installed, in read-only mode, for audit, reporting, or access to legacy documents. This is common in large enterprises during system transitions.

  4. Build a conversion utility for orders: Prior to uninstalling Vistex, enrich and copy all sales orders referencing Vistex agreements into new SAP-native pricing procedures. This allows continuity but requires significant development and validation effort.

  5. Consider temporary retention of Vistex post-migration: Migrate Vistex to RISE only for the purpose of rendering historical orders. You do not create new agreements in Vistex, but keep the component technically installed to support old documents. Decommission later when legacy data is no longer required operationally.

Conclusion: You cannot directly view Vistex-dependent

Best regards

entry.title: Re: Rejected by bank with "back office code is missing" e...
entry.content:
Dear Reena

The error message "Back office code is missing" returned by the bank during a payment via SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (MBC) usually indicates that a required field — often used for internal bank routing, identification, or processing — is not being filled correctly in the payment file (typically in the pain.001 XML message).

This "Back office code" is often a bank-specific mandatory element expected in the payment file, but it's not standard in SAP’s default structure. Here's how to resolve the issue:

  1. Contact your bank to clarify the exact field and XML tag name they refer to as the "Back office code." Common names include <BkCd>, <ProprietaryIdentification>, or fields in <InstrForDbtrAgt>.

  2. Once the bank confirms the XML tag, go to Bank Communication Management (BCM) or Payment Medium Workbench (PMW) and do the following:

    • Identify the payment method and payment format used (e.g., pain.001.001.03).
    • Access the DMEE tree associated with that format (Transaction: DMEE).
    • Extend the DMEE tree to include a node for the "Back office code" at the appropriate structure level (e.g., under PmtInf or CdtTrfTxInf).
    • Map this new node to a custom field in the payment document (e.g., from REGUH, FPAYH, FPAYHX) or to a constant value provided by the bank.

  3. If the bank requires the back office code to be dynamic (e.g., based on company code or payment method), then:

    • Use User Exit EXIT_RFEPOS_PY_001 or BADI FI_PAYMEDIUM_DMEE to fill a custom field.
    • Store the back office code in a custom table or use the vendor/bank master field (like Z* fields or House Bank Account) to hold the value.

  4. Once the DMEE tree is updated:

    • Regenerate the payment file.
    • Retest the payment through MBC.

  5. Finally, verify in T-Code: OBPM3/OBPM4 (Bank Determination settings for BCM) if any custom mapping is needed.

If you're unsure where to map it or if your MBC bank connector requires a specific format like CGI+ or bank proprietary, ask your bank for an example of a valid file that worked with the back office code included.

In short: confirm with the bank the exact tag needed, update the DMEE tree to include it, and map it via exit or custom logic. This will resolve the rejection.

Best regards

entry.title: Re: BOM component loaded wrongly in Routing
entry.content:
Hi

In SAP S/4HANA Migration Cockpit (LTMC), when using the Routing object to assign BOM components, inconsistencies between the ITEM ID, Item Number, and BOM Component can occur if the data mappings in the staging file are not properly aligned. This leads to incorrect assignments in the component allocation of the routing operations.

Since LTMC does not support "update" or "modify" mode for routing component allocations once uploaded, you must use a remediation approach that involves deleting the incorrect entries and reloading the correct ones.

Here’s how you can achieve this:

  1. Delete the Incorrect Routing Entries: Use transaction CA02 (or mass processing with CC62) to manually or programmatically remove the component allocations for the affected routings. If you have many routings, you can use a custom LSMW or BDC to remove the wrong components.

  2. Prepare a Clean Staging File for Correct Upload: In the LTMC template for Routing:

    • Ensure Group Counter, Group, and Operation Number exactly match those used in the existing routings.
    • Under Component Assignment tab, ensure that:
      • Item Number exactly matches the one from the BOM.
      • ITEM ID is consistent and unique per routing line.
      • Component Material Number (BOM component) is aligned with the BOM created for the material.

    You can extract the correct BOM data using CS03 or via tables STPO (component items) and STKO (header) to ensure the match.

  3. Reload Correct Data Using LTMC: Re-import the correct routing with updated component assignments via LTMC.

    • LTMC should match the routing operation via Routing Group, Group Counter, and Operation Number, and will create new allocations.
    • Old incorrect components must be deleted first, otherwise you may end up with duplicates.

  4. Alternative via Direct Update: If the number of routings is huge and you want to automate correction without manual deletion:

    • Use BAPI_ROUTING_CREATE for new routing entries.
    • Or use BAPI_ROUTING_CHANGE if allowed (note: limited fields).
    • For complete control, consider using Direct Input method via program RCPTRA01 (for routing) with custom component allocation logic.

  5. Validation: After remediation, validate routing and BOM consistency using:

    • CA03 (routing display),
    • CS03 (BOM display),
    • And tables: MAPL (routing assignment), PLPO (operations), PLMZ (component allocations).

In short: delete incorrect component assignments, prepare a clean LTMC file with correct Item Numbers and BOM Component mapping, and re-upload. LTMC is strictly additive for routings, so the delete step is essential for data correction.

entry.title: Re: HTTP Content Server all communication via SAP server?
entry.content:
Dear Tilo

The behavior you're observing — where ArchiveLink create and get requests are coming from the end-user client IP instead of the SAP server IP — typically occurs when documents are accessed directly from the SAP GUI and the SAP GUI client handles the HTTP call to the content server. This is by design in some configurations, but it can be changed.

To force all ArchiveLink HTTP communication to go through the SAP NetWeaver ABAP backend — so only the SAP application server communicates with the HTTP Content Server (not the end user's PC) — you need to ensure that the SAP GUI is not directly initiating the content server request.

Here’s how to configure SAP to ensure that the backend server (not the frontend client) performs the ArchiveLink communication:

1. Activate "Access via Backend" Mode

Use the SAP ArchiveLink HTTP protocol backend processing mode by setting the HTTP access mode to server-based in the content repository configuration.

  • Go to transaction OAC0
  • Choose your content repository (e.g., Z1)
  • Ensure the following:
    • Storage type: HTTP Content Server
    • Access via frontend or backend: set to 'Backend' (value = '2')

Important: This is the key setting. By default, it might be set to "Frontend" (value = '1'), which causes the SAP GUI (user PC) to access the HTTP content server directly.

2. Ensure ICM/HTTP is Configured Correctly on SAP NetWeaver

Ensure that the SAP ICM (Internet Communication Manager) is configured to allow outgoing HTTP(S) communication, since the SAP server itself will be making HTTP requests to the content server.

  • Use transaction SMICM
  • Go to Goto > Services and check that HTTP/HTTPS services are active
  • Use SM59 to test HTTP connection (e.g., to your ArchiveLink server)

3. Set the SAP GUI Behavior for Document Access

Sometimes, frontend-based document display is hardcoded in the document class configuration. In such cases:

  • Go to transaction OAA3 or OAG1
  • Check for your document type / document class
  • Look for options like Viewer type or Linkage to Application, and set those that support backend mode

Additionally, if you use DMS (Document Management System), make sure the DMS settings (via DC10) are also pointing to content repositories configured for backend access.

4. Check SAP Notes and Kernel Patch Level

Older systems might not support all the backend-based access logic properly. Ensure your SAP system is up to date and check the following SAP Notes:

  • SAP Note 1498613 – ArchiveLink: Backend access to HTTP content servers
  • SAP Note 2630630 – ArchiveLink: Backend communication not working properly for HTTP content servers

5. Simplify Firewall Rules

Once backend access is configured and verified:

  • You only need to allow HTTP/HTTPS access from the SAP application server IP(s) to the HTTP Content Server
  • The client PCs (end users) no longer require network access to the content server

Final Test

  • After setting OAC0 to “Backend” mode (2), re-test document creation and display via SAP GUI
  • Monitor the access log on the HTTP Content Server
  • You should now see the SAP application server IP as the request source instead of the user’s PC IP

If this still does not work, please confirm:

  • Your SAP GUI version
  • That you're not using a viewer or tool on frontend forcing document rendering locally (e.g., SAPGUI viewer plugins)
  • That content is not cached on frontend

This configuration fully centralizes ArchiveLink traffic through the SAP server and resolves firewall/security concerns.

Best regards

entry.title: Innovating Banking Connectivity with SAP in Portugal – Join Us at SAP Inside Track Porto 2025
entry.content:
Innovating Banking Connectivity with SAP in Portugal – Join Us at SAP Inside Track Porto

We are excited to invite you to a joint session by Makevalue and SIBS at SAP Inside Track Porto. In this session, we will present a real-life case of innovation in banking connectivity using SAP technologies in Portugal.

Over the past few months, the Makevalue team has successfully implemented a suite of banking APIs, creating a modern and fully integrated solution within SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA:

Collections: APIs from SIBS were integrated, enabling seamless use of MB Way and Multibanco References within the SAP environment

Payments: The solution also includes real-time integration with Millennium BCP’s payment APIs, allowing end-to-end automation from SAP to bank systems

From Challenge to Innovation: A 6-Month Integration Journey

This project was delivered over six months and involved multidisciplinary finance, IT, and banking teams. It wasn’t just about technical integration but designing a resilient, scalable process to support day-to-day operations.

The implementation included standard payment and collection flows and advanced financial services such as Risco Sacado (Supplier Confirming), enhancing the organization’s working capital management and financial automation.

What to Expect at the Session

The session will feature project leaders and stakeholders who will share:

  • The journey from concept to go-live

  • Key technical and functional challenges

  • Lessons learned and best practices

  • A live demonstration of the integrated process running in SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA

Why You Should Attend

If you're working with SAP and looking to automate, simplify, and modernize banking communication in Portugal, this session will be of special value. You’ll see how SAP BTP and open banking APIs can work together to reduce manual work, increase control, and deliver strategic benefits to your finance department.

Join us at SAP Inside Track Porto 2025 on 27 September!

SAP Inside Track , #sitPOR , SAP Community

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Time Conversion and Material Cost Ca...
entry.content:
Hello @Mithundogra 

Thanks for raising this, it's a common issue when dealing with very small operation times (milliseconds) and standard cost calculations in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and Product Lifecycle Costing (PLC).

Main causes of Cost Rounding to Zero

In SAP, costing typically works with time units in minutes or hours — even though you can maintain operation time in seconds or milliseconds, the costing engine converts and rounds those values based on standard precision settings.

Key causes of rounding to zero:

Decimal precision in cost calculation

    • Costing in routing/production orders is often based on minutes.
    • Time entries below system-defined decimal thresholds may be treated as 0.000 minutes, especially in standard cost estimates or PLC.

Unit conversion truncation

    • If you're using milliseconds (1 sec = 0.01667 mins), even small rounding during conversion can reduce the effective time to zero.

Costing lot size impact

    • If you're costing per piece and the time per piece is extremely low, total operation cost per unit may round to zero unless the costing lot size is large enough.

Solution

Use Seconds Instead of Milliseconds

    • Switch from ms to seconds, and ensure the operation time is still realistic for the routing.
    • In most standard SAP cost calculations, seconds are the smallest supported effective time unit.

Adjust Lot Size in Costing Variant

    • Increase the costing lot size to amplify the cost impact and avoid rounding to zero.
    • E.g., instead of costing 1 unit, cost for 1,000 or 10,000 units.

Check Decimal Precision Settings

  • Display of decimal places in costing view
  • Costing sheet precision
    • Review your CO (Controlling) and ML (Material Ledger) config under:
    • In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you may need to request a config change via Expert Configuration request (through SAP support).

Product Lifecycle Costing (PLC) Behaviour

  • In SAP PLC, if milliseconds are used in templates, ensure the UOM conversion and cost component settings are appropriately configured. Otherwise, cost rates might get rounded during simulation.

Suggested SAP Notes / References

  • KBA 1865742 – Quantity Value Gets Rounded When Doing Cost Estimation For A Material 
  • SAP Help Portal – Product Cost Planning 

Summary,

Cause

Solution

Millisecond-to-minute loss

Use seconds instead; avoid ms where possible

Cost rounding per unit

Use a higher costing lot size

System decimal limits

Check/adjust the config via SAP support

Let me know if it was helpful

With regards

Chuma

entry.title: Personalized Employee Career Journey with SAP SuccessFactors Career & Talent Development (CTD)
entry.content:
Introducing SAP SuccessFactors Career and Talent Development

Until a few years ago, Career Development used to be a mix of wishful thinking, awkward performance reviews, and a sprinkle of “let’s circle back in six months.” But in today’s fast-moving world, employees want real growth, real opportunities, and real-time guidance. Enter SAP SuccessFactors Career and Talent Development, your AI-powered guided experience for career growth and internal mobility, that helps employees navigate their growth journey while giving organizations the talent agility they need.

With skills-driven insights, personalized career recommendations, and AI-driven insights, this solution is about fueling continuous growth and making talent planning feel less like guesswork and more like a strategic advantage. Whether it’s building the right skills for the future, providing a guided experience tailored to each individual, or empowering employees with easy-to-use tools.

"SAP has introduced SAP SuccessFactors Career and Talent Development (CTD). The solution combines the robust features of the SAP SuccessFactors Succession & Development solution with the SAP SuccessFactors Opportunity Marketplace solution and is powered by skills data from the talent intelligence hub."

Career and Talent Development vs. Succession & Development 

With Career and talent development, the license includes all of the functionality of  Succession & Development plus, OMP, new functionality as of the 2H release, including Experiential opportunities, AI-assisted career insights, visual career exploration, and new role recommendation PLUS a guided experience with the growth portfolio as the starting point for the employee’s development journey.

 Let's dive deeper into these key features available only with SAP SuccessFactors Career & Talent Development (CTD). Navigating career development is no longer a solo journey with CTD; it’s a guided, intuitive experience designed to empower employees with clarity, direction, and personalized opportunities. Here’s how the latest capabilities are shaping an engaging path forward:

1. Guided Experience for Career Development in Growth Portfolio

With an integrated user experience that brings together the Growth Portfolio, Opportunity Marketplace, and the latest Career Worksheet, employees now have one central place to reflect on their skills, interests, and aspirations. From understanding what’s expected in their current role to discovering new opportunities across the organization, everything is now within easy reach.

2. AI-Assisted career insights:

When employees explore a target role, AI-generated career insights help them understand how their profile aligns. These insights offer personalized guidance, helping employees identify development goals and focus areas that can accelerate their journey to the next step.

3. Opportunity marketplace (OMP):

Access to the OMP landing page, opportunity recommendations, opportunity search, manage and track opportunities and experiential opportunities (assignments) from within the Growth Portfolio page.

4. Smarter Role recommendations:

The new role recommendation engine introduces multiple categories of career suggestions, including:

  • Suggested Roles: Based on skills and interests, using logic defined by HR teams

  • AI-Based Roles (Career Explorer): Powered by machine learning to surface relevant paths

  • Trending Roles: Tied to current job openings

  • Career Path Roles: Predefined, structured paths to help employees see what’s next

This helps employees explore meaningful options that align with their aspirations and the company’s evolving needs.

5. Visual Career Exploration:

Visual Career Exploration brings career possibilities to life. With an interactive interface, employees can go beyond linear progressions and discover long-term career paths, adjacent roles, and unexpected opportunities, all mapped out visually to inspire action and curiosity.

Why you should get the new Career and Talent Development solution :

Here are the key reasons you should get the new SKU for Career and Talent Development if you are thinking about Talent Intelligence Hub, or upgrade to the new SKU if you already have Succession and Development.

  • All of the functionalities of Succession & Development and Opportunity Marketplace in one solution
  • Guided experience with Growth Portfolio as the starting point for all development journeys
  • Future innovations are planned exclusively for the Career and Talent development solution only.

NOTE:  There is a new SKU/License for Career and Talent Development. If you currently have Succession and Development, talk to your AE to upgrade to the CTD SKU. If you are starting your Skills journey, start with CTD. When you upgrade from Succession and Development, you don't lose any existing configured functionalities; you get the new features! It is NOT a migration.

Talent Intelligence Hub with Career & Talent Development drives Internal Mobility :

How Talent Intelligence Hub and Career & Talent Development are driving Internal Mobility? Here's how an employee's Career journey will look within the organization with Talent Intelligence Hub, Skills, and Career and Talent Development in place. I recommend the following solutions to turn on and configure to take advantage of Career and Talent Development as part of your skills journey:

  • Talent Intelligence Hub, including Job Profile Builder
  • Latest Career Worksheet

A few other Premium AI features that can assist with wholesome skills-based Internal Mobility :

  • AI-Assisted Skills Architecture Creation

  • Skill Inference from LMS Course, CPM and OMP Assignment
  • AI-assisted Assignment Creation in OMP
  • AI-assisted Career Insights
  • Base AI (included) - Role and Opportunity Recommendation

 

Make each employee a success story, not just by handing them a job title but by empowering them to grow, stretch, and thrive through skills. The Talent Intelligence hub, together with Career and Talent Development, can do this! 

Instead of the same old career ladder, give employees a personalized, AI-guided journey where they can reflect on their strengths, explore future roles, and build the right skills to get there. With the Growth Portfolio as their launchpad, and visual career paths to guide the way, they’ll know exactly where they’re headed and how to get there.

SAP SuccessFactors | Talent Management

SAP SuccessFactors Solutions | Career and Talent Development

entry.title: Short Overview regarding Raw material compliance with EHS Classic
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog I will try to describe the challenge for an e.g. chemical company in handling raw materials (describing a chemistry).;  what are the critical aspects to look at (from EHS point of view and partially from MM (Material) point of view.

The Basics

EHS differentiate in objects of type REAL_SUB and REAL_SUB_E; discussion in https://community.sap.com/t5/product-lifecycle-management-q-a/documentation-on-real-sub-e/qaq-p/7361326 and / or https://community.sap.com/t5/product-lifecycle-management-q-a/why-real-sub-e/qaq-p/10182231

and/or https://community.sap.com/t5/product-lifecycle-management-q-a/real-sub-e-for-supplier-data/qaq-p/10224607

and/or

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/reach-creation-of-real-sub-e-linking-with-real-sub/qaq-p/5547102

and many others showed that the difference between REAL_SUB and REAL_SUB_E is may be not understood very well.

E.g. https://community.sap.com/t5/product-lifecycle-management-q-a/ehs-classic-data-model/qaq-p/12101720 is referring to the SAP standard model which is shared in many lectures.

After some research: we can find this SAP help:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/e3e7c06560a54d14ba7f4c52a86635da/1da3fd53b988e362e10000000a44176d.html?locale=en-US&q=REAL_SUB_E

and https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/e3e7c06560a54d14ba7f4c52a86635da/4a48fb53b9c6161ae10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US&q=REAL_SUB_E

And you will find than this sentence in the SAP online help:

"It is recommended that you use the specification type External Real Substance (REAL_SUB_E) for supplier materials"

But what is now the consequence? How to manage this REAL_SUB_E etc.

The SAP help indicate this:  if you are using a material number for a raw material you should link this Material number to a REAL_SUB_E. What but are the additionaly consequences?

Now let us come back to the challenge as such:

To use SAP ERP (whatever version): any logistic process runs with "material numbers". And the above topic is referring to a "material".

Now let us look on options, how to handle this (assuming you are buying from many suppliers the "same" chemistry (e.g. benzene)).

Framework

At any point in time the supplier can send e.g. a new SDS or other information. So there is a regular need to check the data of the supplier and then to decide: what is the next step (in using the new/changed data).

Several options exists to manage this challenge. Here are the most important options to check:

Case 1: you use a material / EHS model like: any supplier is getting his own material numbers and for any supplier you define a REAL_SUB_E

As a consequence: you need to consider imporant aspects like:

  • You need to generate one material number by supplier (e.g. by may be using the name of the material as used by the supplier)
  • You need a Safety Data Sheet (SDS) from the supplier of the material
  • In most of the cases the information in SDS is not "good" enough to be used in EHS. So you need (what is called) on top a "specification" (or other documens giving more insight regardgin the chemistry); such a specification has many different variants. Many companies use it to describe "general" topics like: does "not contain" aspects or "is registered" (in US, REACH etc:); sometimes you get more data regarding the "Chemistry"
  • Then you wil generate one REAL_SUB_E and link the material number to this object
  • As a consequence: you need to upload the SDS (to the REAL_SUB_E) and the specfication should get some e.g. "identfier" or you can use the specification header remark field to make a link to a "Supplier". This supplier you can describe by a "Name" or (which is much more better) by using a "Business Partner Number"
  • Now by reading the Safety Data Sheet information you can extract the important EHS data and then try to map this to your EHS structure
  • Quite interesting is the challenge: how to use now the REAL_SUB_E if these are "starters" for other material numbers (which you spriduce and then sell) and for which you would prepare a REAL_SUB. How you do the job ? ... based on experience: I am sorry there is no "black", "white"

What is the benefit of this data model: per "material number / supplier" you prepare a REAL_SUB_E. On this object only data from one supplier need to be managed. Only the SDS of this supplier need to be uploaded etc.

You can call this: Simple and clear to understand. But if you have many supplier for the same chemistry: you need more time for maintenance (on REAL_SUB_E Level) and the use of these "REAL_SUB_E" objects is much more complex (as you need this REAL_SUB_E to manage data on other objects).

Case 2: You use the same material numbers for any supplier but still one REAL_SUB_E

This is now more complex (from functional point of view): You need now to read "all" relevant SDS (and other information) and try to generate "one common EHS data set" on REAL_SUB_E level; but it reduces the maintenance on EHS side. It reduces as well the number of material numbers to check/look at. But the "negative" effect is: on the same REAL_SUB_E you now handly many SDS from many suppliers (e.g. by using CG36VEN). The positive effect is: you have one common "REAL_SUB_E" which you can consume in other specifications objects easily.

Case 3: you ignore the recommendation of SAP and maintain all data on REAL_SUB level 

In a nutshell: you do not use REAL_SUB_E but REAL_SUB to do the job,

I will not go here to the details: but this model seems to be used as well.

Why is the decision so important?

The reason why you should be careful in the decision is the next part.

The above listed raw materials and REAL_SUB_E's can be used to e.g. produce a new chemistry either as physical mixing or they are reacting to get new chemistry.

Now the critical part is the "Mixing". As part of the mixing you normally generate a new material number which you now sell.

And if you sell: you need a SDS (and much more).

But how can this be achieved?

E.g you can use 3E Expert rules to generate lot of data for the "selling" product. 

But if the suppliers are dramatically deviating in the e.g. "GHS" Statement: how should you calculate GHS Classifiication / Labeling for the REAL_SUB?

So the challenge is: how to maintain the composition for such cases (on REAL_SUB level of the selling product) to have an full E2E process.

Conclusion

Based on experience: There is no "black" and "white" .. that means: any chemical companies use a slightly different approach to manage the challenge. And the issue comes only up if the "same" chemistry is to be managed.

Some companies are close to the SAP Standard; some far away.  From technical pont of view: no problem. But from functional point of view: the wrong approach can have negative impact on the daily maintenance of specifications.

But if you start an EHS Classic Implementation. This topic is a critcal one. It determines heavily how you must later use EHS Classic to get the most profit.

And this is a "on top" Challenge. Even if the company is writing down a "maintenance guideline" and trainings are given etc. there is always a risk of deviation (in daily practise) and these deviations should be reduced (there should be a monitoring to make sure that the company decision: hwo to maintain the data: is checked reguarly).

What will be the future?

With SAP Product Compliance: in principle a similar framework  is available as in EHS Classic. The challenges are the same. The technical solution is different.

So: if you plan to move to SAP Product Compliance (either still useing EHS Classic or not): make sure that you have a good unerstanding: what is delivered in SAP Product Compliance and what need to to look at and how to use the new data model.

May be check: 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/35751be3d6ee423197492574e016a512/9c64fdb784504c5e8f21e536f2e1d655.html?locale=en-US

and

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/35751be3d6ee423197492574e016a512/b96801d99f6745a0996bbc079085331f.html?locale=en-US

to get more insight regarding this.

entry.title: Re: Bank Guarantee (Work Around)
entry.content:
Hello @sai_kumar1 

Thanks again for your question regarding the Bank Guarantee process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Since the standard Bank Guarantee Management falls under scope item 2NZ, which may not be active in your system, here's a practical workaround using standard features available in the core Finance scope:

Workaround for Bank Guarantees in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Issuing the Bank Guarantee (Contingent Liability)

  • Post a memo or statistical entry using a non-posting G/L account such as Contingent Liabilities – Bank Guarantee.
  • This can be tracked via a statistical internal order or manually maintained for disclosure purposes in the Financial Statement Version (FSV).

When the Bank Guarantee Is Invoked

If the customer calls on the guarantee, the contingent liability needs to be reclassified as a real liability. At this point, you post an entry to reflect the change:

Debit: Contingent Liabilities – BG
Credit: Bank Guarantee Payable (liability G/L)

This shows the obligation has become a real financial liability on the books.

Settlement of the Bank Guarantee

Once the payment is made to the bank to settle the guarantee, record the transaction as follows:

Debit: Bank Guarantee Payable
Credit: Bank / Cash

This clears the liability from the books and reflects the cash outflow.

This approach ensures the liability is properly tracked, disclosed, and settled, even without activating the dedicated 2NZ scope.

SAP Community reference

Statistical Posting is not supported in Cloud, but it's already planned in the roadmap.

With kind regards

Chuma

entry.title: Re: EBS configuration problem
entry.content:
please check if you have assigned the interpretation algorithm to the external transaction code. You have to assign the posting rule AND interpretation algorithm to the external transaction code.
If you have assigned interpretation algorithm 000, the system will not search for any number. If you assign 001 it should work in this particular case.
